svg.text: Bi-Encoder • Cross-Encoder • ColBERT
Three architectures for matching prompts to documents — the speed/quality/storage triangle
1. The big picture — pick your trade-off
Quality
Speed
Storage
Best for
default semantic search
reranking top candidates
production retrieval at scale
2. Bi-Encoder • embed separately, compare vectors
How it works
1. Separate vectors
Prompt and documents are embedded
by the SAME embedding model but independently.
2. Vectors pre-computed
All document vectors are computed offline.
Only the prompt is embedded at query time.
3. ANN search
The vector DB uses ANN (HNSW) to find
docs whose vectors are closest to the prompt.
3. Cross-Encoder • concat first, score the pair
How it works
1. Concatenate
Prompt + document are joined into ONE input.
2. Joint encoding
The model attends across both halves at once,
producing deep contextual interactions.
3. Relevance score
Direct output: a single number from 0 to 1.

g.text: Must run for EVERY (prompt, document) pair at
→ great for reranking ~50 candidates, never for first-pass.

svg.text: Example: "Great places to eat in New York"

g.text: in Times Square to gems in East Village...
stations, one of the oldest in the world...
pizza in DUMBO, dim sum in Sunset Park...
Notice: doc 3 (Brooklyn) outranks doc 1 (Manhattan) — a bi-encoder wouldn't catch this nuance.

svg.text: 4. ColBERT • one vector per token + MaxSim scoring
The compromise (Contextualized Late Interaction Over BERT)
1. Pre-compute like bi-encoder
Document vectors are built offline. Fast at query time.
2. One vector PER TOKEN (not per doc)
Every token gets its own embedding → preserves fine-grained meaning.
3. MaxSim scoring
For each prompt token, find its best-matching doc token,
then sum those maxima → document's overall score.
MaxSim example
Prompt: "Great places to eat in New York" vs. Doc: "The cuisine of New York City comprises many..."
= 3.6 (MaxSim)

g.text: Each prompt token "shops around" for its best doc-token match.
"eat" finds "cuisine" (0.7), "New York" finds "New York" (0.9 + 0.9) — concept-level matching beats average pooling.

svg.text: 5. Key takeaways & what to use in 2026

text.tspan: = default for first-pass semantic search. Cheap, fast, good enough for most queries.
= highest quality, but only viable as a reranker over the top 20–100 candidates.
= nearly cross-encoder quality at bi-encoder-ish speed. Costs storage. Increasingly native in vector DBs.

svg.text: In practice: production stacks use bi-encoder for recall + cross-encoder / ColBERT for the precision pass.
2026 production stack • the two-stage retrieval pattern
● Stage 1 (recall): bi-encoder + ANN (HNSW) pulls top-100 candidates fast. Modern embedders: text-embedding-3-large, Voyage-3, Cohere v3.
● Stage 2 (precision): cross-encoder rerank narrows to top-5. Production rerankers: Cohere Rerank 3, Voyage rerank-2, BGE / Jina rerankers, Mixedbread.
● ColBERT family: ColBERTv2 + PLAID for efficient inference; ColPali brings late-interaction to vision-language. Native support in Vespa, Weaviate, Qdrant.
● Rule of thumb: always start with bi-encoder + reranker. Reach for ColBERT only when you've tuned that and need more.
